nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving tributaries, flooded forest margins, and small streams with dense aquatic vegetation and leaf litter; typically over fine sand or mud substrate with low to moderate current

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java moss, fine-leaved stem plants, floating plants for diffused light)
low-flow areas or gentle filtration output
open mid-water swimming space for shoaling

diet.notes: Micro-omnivore. Accepts high-quality micro-pellets, finely crushed flake, micro wafers, and small frozen/live foods (baby brine shrimp, micro worms, daphnia, cyclops). Sifts substrate for small organic particles. Avoid large pellets — tiny mouth size limits food particle diameter to roughly 0.5–1 mm.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (ember tetras, neon tetras)
dwarf shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina — safe with adults)
avoidWith: large or boisterous fish that outcompete at feeding
large substrate-disturbing species that stress mid-water shoalers

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase specimen image of Corydoras pygmaeus (now Gastrodermus pygmaeus); verify image is current and appropriately attributed before use.

summary: One of the smallest corydoras, reaching only 2–3 cm. Uniquely shoals in mid-water rather than on the bottom. Native to western Amazon tributaries. Easy to keep in groups of 8+ in soft, slightly acidic water with fine sand and gentle flow.
careNotes: The Pygmy Corydoras (Gastrodermus pygmaeus) is one of the tiniest members of the armored catfish family Callichthyidae, topping out at roughly 3 cm. Unlike most corydoras which are bottom-dwellers, this species exhibits a distinctive mid-water shoaling behavior — groups are commonly seen swimming actively in the middle of the water column, especially when kept in appropriate numbers. A shoal of at least 8 individuals (ideally 10–15+) is strongly recommended to bring out this natural behavior and reduce stress; undersized groups often hide and rarely venture from the substrate. A 40–45 L tank is a workable minimum for a small species-focused group, though a larger footprint benefits a larger shoal. Fine sand is essential to protect delicate barbels from abrasion — coarse gravel should be avoided. Soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–15) best matches their western Amazon origin, though they adapt tolerably well to neutral moderately soft water. Gentle filtration is preferred; strong flow stresses this small species. Dense planting with Java moss, fine-leaved plants, and floating cover provides security and diffuses light. Their tiny mouths require appropriately sized micro-foods; standard corydoras sinking wafers are typically too large — use micro-pellets, finely crushed flake, and small frozen foods (baby brine shrimp, micro worms). Like all callichthyid catfishes, this species possesses venomous spines on the pectoral and dorsal fins capable of causing a painful sting if handled carelessly — use a soft mesh net and avoid handling directly. TAXONOMY NOTE (2024 merge candidate): This species was universally known as Corydoras pygmaeus until a 2024 generic revision by Dias & Britto split several dwarf corydoras into the new genus Gastrodermus, renaming this species Gastrodermus pygmaeus. The legacy name Corydoras pygmaeus remains widely used in trade and older aquarium literature. AquaMate entry fw-fish-087 (“Pygmy Cory”, Corydoras pygmaeus) documents the same species under the pre-2024 name and is a MERGE CANDIDATE with this entry (fw-fish-453). The same taxonomic revision also moved the Tail Spot Pygmy Cory (fw-fish-130/fw-fish-372) to Gastrodermus hastatus. Both merges are pending editorial review.
breedingNotes: Spawning follows the typical corydoras T-position method: males fertilize eggs as the female cups them in her pelvic fins before depositing them on plant leaves, the tank glass, or moss. Water changes with slightly cooler water often trigger spawning. Eggs are small (roughly 1 mm) and adhesive; parents may eat eggs if left in the breeding tank. Remove eggs or adults after spawning. Eggs hatch in approximately 3–4 days at 24°C; fry are free-swimming after another 2–3 days and can be fed infusoria, green water, or commercial fry foods. Fry are fragile — pristine water quality is critical. Breeding difficulty is rated moderate due to the care required for egg incubation and fry rearing, though conditioning adults is straightforward.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras pygmaeus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (~2.5 cm SL males, ~3.2 cm SL females), temperature 22–26°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 18–215 ppm (~1–12 dH), native range (upper Rio Madeira system, Brazil), mid-water shoaling behavior, breeding method (T-position, egg scattering on plants), diet (small invertebrates, organic particles), and tank recommendations. Note: species listed under legacy name Corydoras pygmaeus; reflects pre-2024 taxonomy.

additional.name: FishBase — Corydoras pygmaeus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max ~3.2 cm TL), taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes), native range (Amazon basin, Brazil), and diet (invertebrates, organic detritus). Also records venomous pectoral/dorsal fin spines. Listed under Corydoras pygmaeus pending FishBase adoption of 2024 Dias/Britto Gastrodermus revision.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Pygmy Corydoras Care Guide
additional.notes: Hobbyist trade source. Confirmed group size recommendation (6+ minimum, 10+ ideal), mid-water shoaling behavior as a distinguishing trait, fine sand requirement, micro-food diet, tank size guidance (10 gal / ~38 L minimum), and compatibility with small peaceful fish and dwarf shrimp.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Corydoras pygmaeus species profile
additional.notes: UK hobbyist source. Confirmed mid-water shoaling behavior (distinguishes pygmaeus from other dwarf cories), temperature range 22–26°C, soft slightly acidic water preference, minimum group size 8–10, breeding T-position method, and western Amazon (Rio Madeira drainage) origin.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Dwarf Corydoras Species Feature
additional.notes: Cross-referenced dwarf corydoras group (pygmaeus, hastatus, habrosus). Confirmed Corydoras pygmaeus as mid-water shoaler distinct from substrate-dwelling hastatus and habrosus; noted venomous spines across genus, soft-water preference, and micro-food requirement. 2024 Gastrodermus reclassification not yet reflected in available TFH online archive.

fish.breedingNotes: T-position egg scattering on plant leaves, glass, or moss. Cool water change often triggers spawning. Eggs (~1 mm) hatch in 3–4 days; fry require infusoria or commercial fry food. Remove adults or eggs post-spawn to prevent predation. Fry sensitive to water quality.
